chunks.page_summary: This page provides an overview of the Rheonics SRD-X3 inline process density meter with a Tri-Clamp connection, detailing its purpose, related products, availability, and where to find further information regarding Tri-Clamp sizes.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

SRD-X3: Inline Process Density meter with Tri-Clamp connection

Modified on: Mon, 30 Dec, 2024 at 6:20 PM

What products are involved?
(https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/) SRD - Inline density and viscosity meter
(https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/)

What is the purpose of this article?
To give an overview of the SRD with Tri-Clamp connection, installation guidelines, instructions, special considerations and available accessories. Tri-Clamp connection varies in size but this article is valid for all of them.

1. Introduction
SRD is Rheonics sensor for inline density and viscosity measurements. Read more on SRD here (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/).
At Rheonics, SRD with Tri-Clamp connection is typically on stock in different sizes. For some high sizes, less stock is available, so the lead-time for delivery can increase.

See all (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors) (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors) (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors) Tri-Clamp sizes available for Rheonics SRD inline density and viscosity meter. (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors)

See all (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors) (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors) (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors)
key_facts: The SRD-X3 is an inline process density meter that utilizes a Tri-Clamp connection.
This article provides an overview of the SRD with Tri-Clamp connection, covering installation guidelines, instructions, special considerations, and available accessories.
The SRD is a Rheonics sensor used for inline density and viscosity measurements.
Rheonics typically stocks SRD sensors with Tri-Clamp connections in various sizes.
For larger Tri-Clamp sizes, less stock may be available, leading to increased delivery times.
The information in this article is applicable to all Tri-Clamp connection sizes for the SRD.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides technical details, dimensions, and specifications for Rheonics SRD inline density and viscosity meters equipped with Tri-Clamp connections, including standard (SRD-X3-15T) and hygienic (SRD-HS-X3-15T) versions.
product_names: SRD inline density and viscosity meter
chunks.extracted_text:
sensors) CAD models for Rheonics SRD inline density and viscosity meter with Tri-Clamp connection. (https://rheonics.com/cad-type-sr-triclamp/)

A Tri-Clamp or TC fitting allows for an easy process installation in pipes and tanks. It is also widely used in the food industry, and at any place where frequent cleaning and disassembly are required. A TC connection is composed of 3 main parts: the ferrules, a gasket and a clamp. Find out how to select the correct gasket here. Pressure and temperature ratings vary for a Tri-Clamp connection, based on the type of clamp used.

Rheonics offers the SRD-HS-X3 as hygienic density and viscosity meter with 3-A and EHEDG certificates (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000412134-ehedg-and-3-a) available.

Check if your required Tri-Clamp norm and size meets the requirements for Rheonics SRD here (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000411921-tri-clamp-sizes-compatible-with-rheonics-sr-sensors).

chunks.page_summary: This page provides general installation guidelines for Rheonics SR-sensors, emphasizing the importance of complete submersion of the SRD sensing element to ensure accurate density and viscosity measurements, and suggests installation configurations for proper fluid filling and drainage.
chunks.extracted_text:
2. General Installation Guidelines

SR-sensors can be placed at any point in a process and at any orientation but is helpful to consider some terms that are usually involved in the sensor measurements.

2.1 Submersion

The SRD has a sensing element shown inside the red section in Figure 2. This section of the sensor should always be fully submerged in the fluid since SRD measures what is in contact with its sensing element. Incomplete submersion can lead to inaccurate or systematic errors in density and viscosity readings. This is a common issue with unfill pipes due to very low flow rate.

When there is a chance of incomplete pipe filling then consider installing the sensor in a flow cell or section which would ensure the section is full before fluid exits, this can be done by making the outlet higher than the inlet (Figure 3a and 3b). However here consideration should be given to create self-draining installs that minimizes the chances of sedimentation.

Figure 3: SRD sensing area.

Figure 3a shows an installation with the SRD in the IFC-15T-34N-SRD flow cell (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/ifc-15t-34n-srd/), inlet and outlet ports are 3/4" NPT threads.
Figure 3b shows an equivalent alternative with the FET-15T flow elbow tee (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/fet-15t-15t/) from Rheonics which has a shorten 1.5” port for the probe, also available in 2” size.
Both solutions have the inlet below the outlet port to favor the complete immersion of the sensing element in the fluid before it exits the cell.
key_facts: SR-sensors can be placed at any point in a process and at any orientation.
The SRD sensing element, shown inside the red section in Figure 2, must always be fully submerged in the fluid for accurate measurements.
Incomplete submersion of the SRD sensing element can lead to inaccurate or systematic errors in density and viscosity readings.
A common cause of incomplete submersion is unfill pipes due to very low flow rates.
To ensure complete pipe filling, the sensor should be installed in a flow cell or section where the outlet is higher than the inlet.
Consideration should be given to creating self-draining installs to minimize the chances of sedimentation.
The IFC-15T-34N-SRD flow cell's inlet and outlet ports are 3/4" NPT threads.
The FET-15T flow elbow tee offers a shorten 1.5” port for the probe and is also available in 2” size.
Both the IFC-15T-34N-SRD flow cell and FET-15T flow elbow tee solutions are designed to have the inlet below the outlet to ensure complete immersion of the sensing element.
warnings_notes: Incomplete submersion can lead to inaccurate or systematic errors in density and viscosity readings.
Consideration should be given to create self-draining installs that minimizes the chances of sedimentation.

2.2 Stagnation/dead zone
Is not a good practice to have dead or stagnations zones around the sensing area (Figure 3). A stagnation or dead zone is where a fluid transfer is not good and older fluid may not be fully displaced by newer fluid. This leads to incorrect measurements due to part, or all, of the measurements being influenced by stationary fluid that is no longer representative of the actual process fluid.
Consider that some ferrules may cause dead zones in the gaps created with installation long standpipes. Rheonics recommends the use of short ferrules or its WFT-15T ferrule (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/wft-15t.pdf) weldolet. (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/wft-15t.pdf) weldolet.
key_facts: The illustrated flow cells are designed to ensure full immersion of the sensor.
It is not good practice to have dead or stagnation zones around the sensing area.
Stagnation or dead zones hinder good fluid transfer, preventing older fluid from being fully displaced.
Stagnation zones lead to incorrect measurements influenced by stationary fluid that is not representative of the actual process fluid.
Some ferrules and long standpipes can create dead zones due to gaps.
Rheonics recommends using short ferrules or its WFT-15T ferrule/weldolet to avoid dead zones.
warnings_notes: It is not a good practice to have dead or stagnation zones around the sensing area (Figure 3).
A stagnation or dead zone is where fluid transfer is not good, and older fluid may not be fully displaced by newer fluid, leading to incorrect measurements.
Measurements in stagnation zones are influenced by stationary fluid that is no longer representative of the actual process fluid.
Some ferrules and long standpipes can cause dead zones in the gaps created during installation.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides technical recommendations for the installation and orientation of the SRD sensing tip, detailing considerations for fluid flow, especially for Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids, and sensor placement to ensure accurate measurements.
chunks.extracted_text:
A clearance of at least 12mm is recommended between the SRD tip and the pipe wall or any other obstruction.

2.3 Recommended orientation
SRD has a sensing tip that is recommended to be aligned with the fluid flow direction to avoid creating recirculation zones around it. Read more on this topic, here (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000408815-srd-fluid-end-orientation).

Figure 4: SRD sensing tip.

2.4 Flow
For Newtonian fluids, the flow rate does not affect the measurements, so SRD should measure the same viscosity and density values in static and moving states of a fluid at the same temperature.
For non-Newtonian fluids (the vast majority of fluids) the flow rate does matter and viscosity readings may differ between static and moving conditions. Density readings, on the other hand, won't be affected as much as the viscosity reading, and for pure density applications, Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids can be treated similarly.
For processes with non-constant flow rates, the recommendation is to install the SRD in a section in the pipeline with the most consistent flow rate to have steady reading values.
Flow rate is also relevant to ensure the full submersion of the SRD sensing element into the fluid. For a process with a low flow rate, pipes may not be full of fluid at all points, so SRD should then be placed in a section where the pipe is usually full of fluid (i.e. after a pump). Installing the sensor upwards can also solve the issue but should not be considered for fluids with solids or that can create deposits around the sensing element.
In all cases, the SRD’s sensing element should be submerged in the fluid. It’s best to avoid installations with long standpipes (i.e. using long weldolet or tee), since that may lead to not good fluid transfer resulting in worse high noise measurements.
When installing in a standpipe, choose the appropriate insertion length of the sensor by selecting long insertion probes. This allows the sensing element to clear the stagnation zone and be in the fluid that is of interest for measurement, as in the next figure.
key_facts: A clearance of at least 12mm is recommended between the SRD tip and any obstruction.
The SRD sensing tip should be aligned with the fluid flow direction to avoid creating recirculation zones.
For Newtonian fluids, the flow rate does not affect viscosity and density measurements; they should be the same in static and moving states at a given temperature.
For non-Newtonian fluids, flow rate matters for viscosity readings, which may differ between static and moving conditions.
Density readings for non-Newtonian fluids are less affected by flow rate compared to viscosity readings.
For pure density applications, Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids can be treated similarly.
For processes with non-constant flow rates, the SRD should be installed in a pipeline section with the most consistent flow rate to ensure steady reading values.
Full submersion of the SRD sensing element into the fluid is crucial.
In processes with low flow rates where pipes might not always be full, the SRD should be placed in a section typically full of fluid (e.g., after a pump).
When installing in a standpipe, choose an appropriate long insertion probe to ensure the sensing element clears the stagnation zone and is in the fluid of interest for measurement.
warnings_notes: Installing the sensor upwards should not be considered for fluids with solids or those that can create deposits around the sensing element.
It is best to avoid installations with long standpipes (e.g., using long weldolet or tee) as this may lead to poor fluid transfer and worse high noise measurements.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides an illustration of sensor installation in a standpipe, details about suitable fluid types for the SRD sensor, and an introduction to process connection installation instructions.
chunks.extracted_text:
non movement zone

Measurement zone
Stand pipe
A: Insertion length

Figure 5: Long insertion probes for long standpipes.

2.5 Fluid types
SRD has good performance with the following fluid types and scenarios:

*   Static and moving Newtonian fluids
*   Moving non-Newtonian fluids: Viscosity can vary at different flow rates, SRD needs to be fully submerged and without stagnation zones.
*   Slurries: Typical use case for the SRD. Density in slurries vary with time, as slurry ages, and as it is mixed.
*   Fluids with solids of micrometers scale: Solids of the size of rice corn shouldn't affect measurements.
*   Fluids with greater solids: Solids with a bigger size will most likely create spikes in the SRD readings when these hit the sensor. Depending on the percentage of solids in the fluid and after some data analysis, Rheonics may be able to apply some filters to handle these fluids better. However, it is hepful to avoid this kind of solids or install the sensor axially.

3. Process connection installation instructions
3.1 Connection steps
A Tri-Clamp connection requires two ferrules or flanges, a seal and a clamp, as in the next figure.
key_facts: SRD sensors are suitable for long insertion probes in long standpipes.
The SRD sensor performs well with static and moving Newtonian fluids.
For moving non-Newtonian fluids, the SRD sensor must be fully submerged and free of stagnation zones.
Slurries are a typical use case for the SRD, where density can vary with time, as the slurry ages, and as it is mixed.
Solids on the micrometers scale, such as rice corn, should not affect SRD measurements.
Larger solids can cause spikes in SRD readings if they hit the sensor.
Rheonics may apply filters to manage fluids with greater solids after data analysis.
A Tri-Clamp connection requires two ferrules or flanges, a seal, and a clamp.
It is hepful to avoid certain types of solids or install the sensor axially when dealing with fluids that have greater solids.
warnings_notes: SRD needs to be fully submerged and without stagnation zones for moving non-Newtonian fluids.
Solids with a bigger size will most likely create spikes in the SRD readings when these hit the sensor.
It is hepful to avoid this kind of solids or install the sensor axially to prevent issues with greater solids.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides detailed installation instructions for Rheonics sensors using Tri-Clamp connections, covering general assembly steps, and specific guidance for inline, in-tank, and long insertion probe applications, alongside important considerations for hygienic installations.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 6: Tri-Clamp connection components.

The general steps for a Tri-Clamp connection are:
i. Have a welded ferrule in the pipe or tank.
ii. Place the gasket properly into the seat of the ferrule.
iii. Place the sensor Tri-Clamp connection on the gasket and verify it is correctly aligned.
iv. Loosen the clamp so it can fit around the ferrule.
v. Place the clamp, verify ferrules are within the clamp's groove.
vi. Start tightening the clamp until a leak-proof connection is made.

Be careful not to over-tighten the clamp, this can damage the gasket and ferrules.

3.2 Specific Process Connection installation instructions

*   In line: For inline installations consider the sensing area (Figure 3) and a minimum clearance of the sensor tip to the pipe inner wall (12 mm). A perpendicular Tri-Clamp installation of the SRD is not possible for most 1”, 1.5” or 2” pipes (for 2” it depends on the pipe norm). In those cases, the installation is recommended to be parallel or with a flow cell, check accessories FET-15T and IFC-15T-SRD. For bigger lines the sensor can be mounted perpendicular to the flow, Rheonics recommends the WFT-15T ferrule for such cases.
*   In tank: Place the sensor at the bottom, wall or lid of the tank as long as the sensing element can be fully submerged in the fluid and dead zones are avoided. The WFT-15T ferrule is recommended for these installations. Usually, it is useful if the sensor is placed at a tank’s height that has always fluid. If there is a hygienic requirement, the SRD-TC can be ordered with a hygienic design as in Figure 1 (optional EHEDG and/or 3-A certification) and should be mounted with a hygienic adapter (e.g. flush welding flange like ISO 1127).
*   Others: Larger pipelines or tanks often need a longer probe (i.e. jacketed tanks or pipes), in those scenarios, the client should use a long insertion probe (FPC sensors) - Order code: SRD-X5.

For special applications contact the Rheonics Team.
key_facts: Tri-Clamp connection requires a welded ferrule in the pipe or tank.
The gasket must be placed properly into the seat of the ferrule.
The sensor Tri-Clamp connection should be placed on the gasket and correctly aligned.
The clamp should be loosened to fit around the ferrule, then placed with ferrules within its groove, and tightened for a leak-proof connection.
For inline installations, consider the sensing area (Figure 3) and ensure a minimum 12 mm clearance from the sensor tip to the pipe inner wall.

A perpendicular Tri-Clamp installation of the SRD is not possible for most 1", 1.5" or 2" pipes; parallel installation or with a flow cell is recommended, or mounting perpendicular to flow with WFT-15T ferrule for bigger lines.
For in-tank installations, the sensor should be placed at the bottom, wall, or lid of the tank, ensuring full submersion of the sensing element and avoidance of dead zones.
The WFT-15T ferrule is recommended for in-tank installations, especially if the sensor is placed where fluid is always present.
SRD-TC sensors can be ordered with EHEDG and/or 3-A certified hygienic designs and mounted with a hygienic adapter for hygienic requirements.
For larger pipelines or jacketed tanks/pipes, a long insertion probe (FPC sensors) with order code SRD-X5 is available.
warnings_notes: Be careful not to over-tighten the clamp, this can damage the gasket and ferrules.

key_facts: The sensing element of the SRD must be sufficiently immersed in the fluid for correct operation.
Insufficient immersion of the sensing element can lead to potential plugging or deposits in the gap.
For pipes 2 inches or smaller, recommended SRD models are FET-15T-XXX, FET-20T-XXX, and FET-15T-15T.
For pipes 2.5 inches or bigger, recommended SRD models are WFT-15T and FTP-15T-XXX.
Using adapters to install an SRD with a different process connection (e.g., NPT) into a Tri-Clamp port is not recommended.
Rheonics SRD sensors should be ordered with the correct process connection to allow direct inline mounting, avoiding the need for additional adapters.
Incorrect adapters, such as NPT to Tri-Clamp, can create stagnant zones and deposits in the sensing area, affecting performance.

Potential plugging or deposits in gap
Stagnant zones Deposits in sensing area

warnings_notes: Important: Avoid using adapters to install the SRD in a Tri-Clamp port when the probe has a different process connection e.g. threaded.
Rheonics SRD should be ordered with the correct process connection to directly mount it inline without the need for additional adapters that can affect the correct immersion of the sensing element.
Not enough clearance for SRD (min. 12 mm) and sensing element not immersed enough are issues to avoid.
Sensing element not immersed enough can lead to potential plugging or deposits in the gap.
Using NPT to Tri-Clamp adapters is not recommended as it can create stagnant zones and deposits in the sensing area.

chunks.page_summary: This page details installation examples and accessories for Rheonics SRD Tri-Clamp sensors, specifically describing the WFT-15T weldolet and IFC-15T-SRD flow cell.
chunks.extracted_text:
Rheonics SRD Tri-Clamp Installation examples
rheonics
Watch on
4. Accessories available
4.1 Weldolet - WFT-15T
Weldolet for 1.5” Tri-Clamp sensor connection that ensures maximum submersion of the sensing element. Suitable for the SRD X3 1.5”. It can be used for process integration into Process lines, Mixing vessels, reactors, etc. Review its web page (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/wft-15t/) for more information.
Figure 9: WFT-15T ferrule
4.2 Flow cells
a. IFC-15T-SRD
Inline adapter for 1.5” Tri-Clamp sensor with 3/4” NPT inlet and outlet ports. This accessory is recommended for DN5 up to DN25 pipes, ensuring optimal flow over the sensing element and minimal stagnation zones. Review its web page (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/ifc-15t-34n-srd/) for more information.
key_facts: The WFT-15T is a weldolet designed for a 1.5” Tri-Clamp sensor connection to ensure maximum submersion of the sensing element.
The WFT-15T weldolet is suitable for the SRD X3 1.5” sensor and can be used for process integration in process lines, mixing vessels, and reactors.
The IFC-15T-SRD is an inline adapter for a 1.5” Tri-Clamp sensor, featuring 3/4” NPT inlet and outlet ports.
The IFC-15T-SRD accessory is recommended for DN5 up to DN25 pipes to ensure optimal flow over the sensing element and minimal stagnation zones.
Detailed information for both the WFT-15T and IFC-15T-SRD accessories is available on the Rheonics product accessories web pages.

chunks.page_summary: This page showcases two different Tri-Clamp elbow tee accessories for Rheonics inline viscometers and density sensors, detailing their connection types and sizes.

Figure 10: IFC-15T-SRD
b. FET-15T-XXX
Tri-Clamp elbow tee for 1.5” Tri-Clamp sensor with welding inlet and outlet ports of 1.5” size. Also available for other line sizes on request. Review its accessory page here (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/fet-15t-15t/).
Figure 11: FET-15T-XXX
c. FET-15T-15T
Tri-Clamp elbow tee for 1.5” Tri-Clamp sensor (DN25/32/40) DIN11850 with same connection for inlet and outlet ports. Review its accessory page here (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/fet-15t-15t/).
key_facts: The IFC-15T-SRD is a Tri-Clamp elbow tee designed for a 1.5-inch Tri-Clamp sensor and features 1.5-inch welding inlet and outlet ports.
The IFC-15T-SRD is available for other line sizes upon request.
The FET-15T-15T is a Tri-Clamp elbow tee for 1.5-inch Tri-Clamp sensors, supporting DN25, DN32, and DN40 sizes according to DIN11850, and has the same connection for both inlet and outlet ports.
Detailed accessory information for both products can be found on the Rheonics product accessories webpage at https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/fet-15t-15t/.
